landing.tagline: Simulate catastrophic events on an interactive 3D globe.
landing.description: An open-source scientific simulator of asteroid impacts, explosions, earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, and tsunamis — rigorous formulas, cinematic comparison scenes, curated for the scientifically curious.

multiEvent.name: Multi-event cascading
multiEvent.detail: An ocean impact triggers tsunami, shock waves, and thermal pulse — simulated in parallel on the same timeline.

realScience.name: Real scientific formulas
realScience.detail: Validated against Collins/Melosh/Marcus 2005, Glasstone & Dolan, Ward & Asphaug, Mastin, USGS ShakeMap.

globe.name: 3D Earth globe
globe.detail: Cesium-powered photorealistic globe for locating events, with damage-ring overlays at true geographic scale.

accessible.name: Accessible and multilingual
accessible.detail: WCAG AA, full keyboard navigation, Italian and English from day one.

shareable.name: Shareable URLs
shareable.detail: Every simulation serialisable in a URL — copy, share, replay.

footer.credits: Inspired by MetaBallStudios · Assets from Poly Haven and Sketchfab CC0 · Science from peer-reviewed literature

simulator.title: Event simulator
simulator.clickGlobe: Click anywhere on the globe to pick the event location.
simulator.monteCarloFooter: Inputs sampled from published distributions (log-normal / normal); P10–P90 bracket the 80 % confidence band.
simulator.impactSummaryHeading: Cosmic impact · energy, crater, damage rings

entryRegimeExplain.INTACT: The body is too massive to dissolve in atmosphere. Even though fragmentation starts at the breakup altitude, the pancake of fragments has so much momentum that it punches through and slams into the ground intact. All the kinetic energy becomes crater + ejecta + seismic work — Chicxulub class.
entryRegimeExplain.PARTIAL_AIRBURST: The pancake bursts in the lower atmosphere (5–15 km). Part of the energy disperses as a thermal flash and shock wave; part still excavates a small crater on the ground.
entryRegimeExplain.COMPLETE_AIRBURST: The pancake disintegrates above 15 km. ≈ 98 % of the kinetic energy is released as a fireball and shock pulse in the air — only ≈ 2 % reaches the ground. Tunguska 1908 and Chelyabinsk 2013 are the canonical examples.

tsunamiDamage.tier0: Negligible — only tide-gauge signature; no infrastructure damage expected (Bryant 2014).
tsunamiDamage.tier1: Light flooding — people can be swept off their feet, light boats displaced, ground floors splashed.
tsunamiDamage.tier2: Significant — cars carried away, single-storey buildings flooded, wood-frame interiors gutted (FEMA P-646 §3).
tsunamiDamage.tier3: Severe — wood-frame buildings destroyed, harbours wrecked, cars driven hundreds of metres inland.
tsunamiDamage.tier4: Catastrophic — single-storey concrete buildings damaged, coastal towns gutted, fatalities high without evacuation.
tsunamiDamage.tier5: Extreme — Tōhoku-/Lituya-class destruction; reinforced concrete and steel-frame buildings collapse, terrain remodelled.

explosion.summaryHeading: Detonation · yield, blast wave, thermal pulse

volcano.summaryHeading: Volcanic eruption · VEI, plume, pyroclastic flows

landslide.summaryHeading: Submarine landslide · volume, geometry, tsunami source

cascade.label: Cascade timeline
cascade.scaleNote: Stages are revealed on a logarithmic time axis (seconds → millennia compressed into ~5 s of UI time). Onset values printed next to each stage are the literal physical onsets, not the on-screen reveal time.

flash.name: Fireball flash
flash.desc: Luminous plasma column at the impact point; intense thermal pulse radiates outward.

crater.name: Crater excavation
crater.desc: Transient cavity carved, then collapsed to the final rim-to-rim diameter.

seismic.name: Seismic wave release
seismic.desc: Ground shock radiates as P/S waves at crustal speeds (6–8 km/s).

airblast.name: Far-field air blast
airblast.desc: Overpressure wave travels at the speed of sound; reaches the 1 psi ring.

ejecta.name: Ballistic ejecta deposit
ejecta.desc: Continuous ejecta blanket falls back over the proximal region.

firestorm.name: Thermal firestorm
firestorm.desc: Kindling ignites inside the ignition radius; convective column develops.

tsunami.name: Tsunami wavefront
tsunami.desc: Water-cavity collapse radiates a long-wave tsunami across the basin.

ejectaReentry.name: Global ejecta re-entry
ejectaReentry.desc: Sub-orbital hot rock re-enters worldwide; the upper atmosphere reaches > 1500 K and broadleaf vegetation ignites well beyond the line-of-sight thermal pulse (Toon et al. 1997 §3; Goldin & Melosh 2009).

stratDust.name: Stratospheric dust peak
stratDust.desc: Sub-µm aerosols distribute globally; solar flux attenuates.

acidRain.name: Acid-rain washout
acidRain.desc: Shock-produced HNO₃ precipitates as acidic rainfall over weeks (Prinn & Fegley 1987).

impactWinter.name: Impact winter
impactWinter.desc: Soot, sulfate aerosols and dust cut surface insolation by an order of magnitude; coupled-climate models put continental cooling at -26 °C for 3-4 years post-K-Pg (Robertson et al. 2013, Brugger et al. 2017).

climate.name: Climate cooling onset
climate.desc: Surface temperatures begin a multi-year decline as the soot lifetime in the stratosphere stretches the cooling forcing past the year-one peak.

photoCollapse.name: Photosynthesis collapse
photoCollapse.desc: Soot optical depth above 50 halts photosynthesis at sea level for ≥ 1 yr; the K-Pg fern-spore spike documents the months-to-years food-chain failure (Pope 2002, Schulte et al. 2010).

oceanAcidification.name: Ocean acidification
oceanAcidification.desc: Sulfate and nitrate aerosols dissolve into the surface ocean; the boron-isotope record at K-Pg shows a ≈ 0.25-unit pH drop within decades (Ohno et al. 2014).

planktonCollapse.name: Plankton collapse
planktonCollapse.desc: Marine primary productivity falls and the calcareous-nannoplankton record bottlenecks. The K-Pg "Strangelove ocean" interval lasts ≈ 0.5–3 Myr; its onset is the first few years (Vellekoop et al. 2014, Lowery et al. 2018).

co2Warming.name: CO₂ rebound warming
co2Warming.desc: Sulfate aerosols rain out within a decade, but volatilised carbonate-platform CO₂ persists. Tunisia foraminifera record +5 °C warming for ≈ 100 kyr post-K-Pg (MacLeod et al. 2018).

massExtinction.name: Mass-extinction marker
massExtinction.desc: Chicxulub-class events drive 70–75 % species turnover within ≈ 10 kyr; ⁴⁰Ar/³⁹Ar dating ties the K-Pg boundary to the impact within 32 ± 5 kyr (Schulte et al. 2010, Renne et al. 2013).

liquefaction.name: Seismic liquefaction ring
liquefaction.desc: Impact-driven Mw triggers Youd-Idriss liquefaction on saturated sandy soils surrounding the crater.

flash.name: Fireball flash
flash.desc: Isotropic thermal pulse reaches its peak within milliseconds.

crater.name: Ground crater
crater.desc: Surface-burst excavation on the selected ground type.

airblast.name: Blast wave arrival
airblast.desc: Overpressure wave travels at ~343 m/s to the 1 psi ring.

firestorm.name: Urban firestorm
firestorm.desc: Ignition of flammable surfaces merges into a self-drawing fire.

falloutPlume.name: Fallout plume drift
falloutPlume.desc: Mushroom-cloud debris drifts downwind and settles over days.

tsunami.name: Underwater-burst tsunami
tsunami.desc: Mechanical coupling of the burst to the water column drives a Glasstone-class wave train across the basin.

nuclearWinter.name: Nuclear-winter onset
nuclearWinter.desc: Multi-megaton groundbursts loft soot and sulphate aerosol into the stratosphere. The Robock-Oman-Stenchikov (2007) regional-exchange model puts 5 Tg of stratospheric soot at 1.25 °C global cooling for ≈ 4 years.

rupture.name: Fault rupture
rupture.desc: Slip propagates along the fault plane at ~3 km/s.

shaking.name: Strong ground motion
shaking.desc: MMI contours expand outward as S-waves sweep the surface.

teleseismic.name: Teleseismic arrival
teleseismic.desc: P-wave first arrival at 1 000 km within ~2–3 minutes.

aftershocks.name: Aftershock sequence
aftershocks.desc: Reasenberg-Jones / Omori-Utsu cascade: hundreds of smaller events decay over the following weeks, capped at M_main − 1.2 by Båth's law.

tsunamiRisk.name: Tsunami risk (submarine)
tsunamiRisk.desc: Large shallow thrust / normal fault can generate a basin-scale tsunami.

tsunami.name: Seismic tsunami wavefront
tsunami.desc: Megathrust coseismic seafloor uplift seeds a cylindrical-spread tsunami propagating across the basin.

tsunamiRunup.name: Coastal run-up
tsunamiRunup.desc: Synolakis 1:100-slope run-up amplifies the open-ocean amplitude by an order of magnitude at the shore.

vent.name: Vent opening
vent.desc: Magma column depressurises at the surface; first gas-rich jet.

plume.name: Plinian column
plume.desc: Buoyant plume rises to the computed height, injecting ash and gas.

pdc.name: Pyroclastic density current
pdc.desc: Hot, ground-hugging flow descends at 100+ km/h to the runout distance.

lateralBlast.name: Lateral blast
lateralBlast.desc: Flank decompression sweeps a directional sector at hundreds of metres per second (Mt St Helens 1980 archetype).

tsunami.name: Volcanic-collapse tsunami
tsunami.desc: Flank or caldera collapse displaces the surrounding water column, seeding a Watts-class wave train (Anak Krakatau 2018, Krakatau 1883 archetypes).

ashfall.name: Regional ashfall
ashfall.desc: Coarse ash settles within hours; fine ash drifts downwind for days.

aerosol.name: Stratospheric aerosol cooling
aerosol.desc: SO₂ / sulfate aerosols spread globally; surface temperatures drop for 1–2 years.

yearWithoutSummer.name: Year without a summer
yearWithoutSummer.desc: VEI 7+ events suppress Northern Hemisphere temperatures by ≈ 0.5–1 °C for 2–3 years; Tambora 1815 triggered the 1816 famine across Europe and North America (Self & Rampino 1981, Oppenheimer 2003).

failure.name: Slope failure
failure.desc: Mass-failure plane releases the block; downslope motion begins.

runout.name: Slide runout
runout.desc: Block accelerates downslope, displacing the surrounding water column.

tsunami.name: Landslide tsunami
tsunami.desc: Watts-class wave train propagates from the source across the basin (Storegga / Anak Krakatau archetypes).

methodology.title: Methodology & scientific citations
methodology.subtitle: Every formula the Nimbus engine evaluates, with its peer-reviewed source and the equation-level DOI. Grouped by event type and paired at the bottom with the roster of historically validated events.
methodology.overviewBody: Nimbus is a headless TypeScript physics engine (Layer 2) wrapped by a Cesium globe (Layer 3) and a React UI (Layer 4). Layer 2 has zero runtime dependencies on React / Three / Cesium — the ESLint configuration blocks such imports so the physics stays testable from a Node CLI. Each formula below lives in its own file under src/physics/ with a JSDoc block that names the citation and the specific equation number used. Tolerances in the test suite honour the scatter published in the original papers — ±30 % for thermal-fluence thresholds, ±50 % for volcanic plume heights, ±factor-2 for ejecta and lahar runouts.
methodology.validationBody: Fifteen of the best-instrumented events in the historical record are re-simulated by the test suite on every commit. When a physics refactor drifts the output outside the published tolerance, the integration test fails first.

uncertainty.title: Uncertainty quantification
uncertainty.body: Every uncertain input has a documented σ in src/physics/uq/conventions.ts, every output has a documented σ in src/physics/confidence.ts, and the Monte-Carlo engine samples 200 realisations to draw P10/P90 bands on the headline metrics. The methodology is documented under docs/SCIENCE.md and the script `pnpm sensitivity` produces an OAT (one-at-a-time) sensitivity report for every preset.
uncertainty.inputNote: Each MC wrapper imports its scatter parameters from a single typed dictionary that names the source paper for the σ value. Examples: impactor diameter σ_log = 0.15 (Mainzer 2019), explosion yield σ_log = 0.1 (Sublette FAQ), Vs30 site velocity σ = 30 % (Wald & Allen 2007).
uncertainty.outputNote: Mirror dictionary OUTPUT_SIGMA documents the published per-quantity scatter that the UI uses to render confidence bars next to the radius numbers. Largest bands: Wünnemann far-field tsunami (factor-3), Mastin plume height (factor-2), pyroclastic runout (±70 %).
uncertainty.mcNote: Mulberry32-seeded so a shareable URL reproduces bit-identical percentiles. The N=200 default is bench-tested against an N=2000 reference: P10/P90 land within 15 %, medians within 10 %.
uncertainty.sensitivityNote: `pnpm sensitivity` runs each input ±1σ and reports per-(input, output) elasticity coefficients. For Tunguska the energy is 80 % driven by impactor diameter (E ∝ d³), 20 % by velocity (E ∝ v²) — useful both for explanatory text and as a regression aid.

regression.title: Predicted-vs-observed regression suite
regression.body: Every commit re-runs four validation tests against published observations of historical events. Each tolerance band is sourced from the underlying model envelope so the contract is honest, not curve-fitted. Full table in docs/VALIDATION.md.
regression.tsunamiNote: Trans-Pacific Tōhoku 2011 + trans-Indian Sumatra 2004 DART / tide-gauge arrivals. RMSE under 60 min, every station matched within ±20 % of the predicted travel time + 3σ observation uncertainty.
regression.shakemapNote: Northridge 1994 + L'Aquila 2009 macroseismic surveys. Cross-event aggregate bias under ±25 %.
regression.plumeNote: Pinatubo 1991, Mount St Helens 1980, Krakatau 1883. Mastin 2009 fit envelope plus the per-event observation σ.
regression.tunguskaNote: Preset KE must lie inside Boslough & Crawford 2008 + Chyba 1993 reconstruction window (3-30 Mt). Catches preset drift or silent formula edits.

limitations.title: Limitations — what Nimbus does NOT model
limitations.body: Honest disclosure of what the simulator deliberately does not capture. None of these are bugs; they are scope decisions a real operational hazard tool would address with a different engine.

atm.note: Plume dispersion uses a 2D Suzuki / Bonadonna analytical envelope, not a 3D Lagrangian particle model. Real ash advection over multiple days needs HYSPLIT / FALL3D — out of scope here.

shore.note: Lamb 1932 + Green 1838 + Synolakis 1987 are linear shallow-water models that break down where the wave breaks. We surface a runup amplitude but do NOT solve the full Boussinesq inundation.

fault.note: Earthquake outputs use a point source + Wells & Coppersmith 1994 area scaling. We do not solve the elastic dislocation field — Okada 1985 strain output, surface fault traces, and stress changes triggering aftershocks beyond the Reasenberg & Jones 1989 fit are out of scope.

plume.note: The 3D mushroom cloud rendered in the Stage view follows the Mastin height scaling but its visual shape and texture are pictorial — a qualitative representation, not a quantitative fluid simulation. The ash-fallout map is the rigorous output, not the cloud.

cascade.item: Climate cascade beyond the first year
cascade.note: Long-term cascade stages (impact winter, ocean acidification, plankton collapse) report literature-derived order-of-magnitude estimates rather than full coupled atmosphere-ocean simulations. Treat the long-term tail as descriptive, not predictive.

methodology.footer: Open-source under Apache 2.0. Corrections and additional citations are welcome via GitHub pull requests.

population.label: Estimated population exposed
population.unavailable: Population data unavailable — set VITE_POPULATION_COG_URL to a CORS-enabled WorldPop / GHSL mirror to enable.
population.disclaimer: Exposure ≠ casualties. This is the population sum inside the damage radius — converting to casualties requires a vulnerability function (Glasstone §12 for blast, Wald & Quitoriano 1999 for shaking) which is intentionally out of scope.

legend.heading: Ring legend
legend.subheading: Click a row to hide or show the matching ring on the globe.
legend.empty: Click a point on the globe and press Simulate to reveal damage rings.
legend.uncertaintyNote: Each radius is a sharp line on the map but a band in reality: published 1σ scatter is ±15–30 % for blast and seismic rings, factor-2 for plumes and far-field tsunami. See the methodology page for the per-quantity envelope.

tsunamiStatus.globalActive: Tsunami map: global + local layer active — trans-oceanic propagation rendered.
tsunamiStatus.localOnly: Tsunami map: local layer only (~150 km) — global mosaic computation failed; check console.
tsunamiStatus.globalLoading: Tsunami map: local layer only — global mosaic still loading; press Launch again in a moment.

aftershockDetail.title: Aftershock detail
aftershockDetail.description: Felt-intensity contours computed for this aftershock alone using the same Joyner-Boore (1981) attenuation and Worden et al. (2012) MMI conversion as the mainshock. Click the globe elsewhere to dismiss.

craterRim.title: Impact crater
craterRim.description: The ground inside this boundary was vaporised and excavated by the impact. There is no surface left here — only a depression of molten rock and glassy debris. Nothing organic survives.

thirdDegreeBurn.title: Third-degree burns
thirdDegreeBurn.description: The thermal flash in this radius is intense enough to cause full-thickness burns on exposed skin, even from a single instant of exposure. Dry wood, fabric and leaves ignite spontaneously.

secondDegreeBurn.title: Second-degree burns
secondDegreeBurn.description: Exposed skin blisters and the underlying dermis is destroyed (5 cal/cm² Glasstone threshold). Painful, often disabling burns; survivable in healthy adults with prompt medical care, but in mass-casualty conditions outcomes degrade rapidly.

overpressure5psi.title: Blast wave · 5 psi
overpressure5psi.description: The peak pressure here (≈ 34 kPa above ambient) collapses reinforced-concrete residential buildings and demolishes unreinforced masonry. People in the open suffer severe injuries from flying debris and ground impact.

overpressure1psi.title: Blast wave · 1 psi
overpressure1psi.description: The pressure here (≈ 7 kPa above ambient) shatters window glass and tears down lightweight structures like awnings and sheds. Rarely lethal, but flying glass causes extensive lacerations near windows.

lightDamage.title: Blast wave · 0.5 psi
lightDamage.description: The outermost reach of the shock front (≈ 3.4 kPa above ambient). Scattered window panes still break, especially on the side facing the blast; shopfronts and unbraced signs may collapse. Personal injury rare except from flying glass.

radiationLD50.title: Initial radiation · LD₅₀
radiationLD50.description: Within this radius the prompt gamma + neutron dose from the fireball is high enough that, without medical intervention, about half of the unshielded people present would die from acute radiation syndrome over the following weeks.

empAffected.title: Electromagnetic pulse
empAffected.description: The early-time E1 spike (Glasstone §11) couples into long power and signal lines. Unhardened consumer electronics inside this footprint are at risk of permanent damage; the actual outcome depends on cable lengths and surge protection. For high-altitude detonations the footprint can be continental.

ejectaBlanket.title: Ejecta blanket
ejectaBlanket.description: Fragments hurled into the air by the impact land here as a layer of fused stones and shattered rock. Thickness at this rim is about 1 mm; closer to the crater it exceeds a metre. Residual heat sets off spontaneous fires.

tsunamiCavity.title: Tsunami source cavity
tsunamiCavity.description: Water has been violently displaced, forming a transient depression whose collapse generates the tsunami wave. Crest height decays with distance (1/r law), but the wave can remain devastating thousands of km away.

tsunamiWaveFront5m.title: Wave front · ≈ 5 m at the coast
tsunamiWaveFront5m.description: At this distance the tsunami still slams the coast at five metres above the high-tide mark. Concrete buildings of two-three storeys can be torn off their foundations; coastal forests are flattened; harbours are rebuilt from scratch. Order of magnitude of Tōhoku 2011 in front of Sendai.

tsunamiWaveFront1m.title: Wave front · ≈ 1 m at the coast
tsunamiWaveFront1m.description: A one-metre wave is enough to knock a person off their feet, sweep cars away and flood ground-floor rooms. Wooden buildings on direct exposure are damaged; reinforced concrete generally survives. Boats moored along the quayside are torn from their lines.

tsunamiWaveFront03m.title: Wave front · ≈ 30 cm at the coast
tsunamiWaveFront03m.description: Even a thirty-centimetre tsunami can drag a person out to sea — water moving at several metres per second carries far more momentum than a same-height river. Tide gauges register a clear signature, evacuations are still recommended, but structural damage is minor.

tsunamiIsochrone1h.title: Tsunami arrival · ≈ 1 h
tsunamiIsochrone1h.description: The wave front reaches this contour about an hour after the source event. On a real coast this is the very edge of the evacuation window — automatic warnings, sirens and SMS alerts must already have been triggered.

tsunamiIsochrone2h.title: Tsunami arrival · ≈ 2 h
tsunamiIsochrone2h.description: Two-hour contour. Time enough to evacuate towns near the coast — but only if the warning chain works. The Lisbon 1755 tsunami reached Cádiz in roughly this time.

tsunamiIsochrone4h.title: Tsunami arrival · ≈ 4 h
tsunamiIsochrone4h.description: The wave front has now travelled across most of an ocean basin. The 2011 Tōhoku tsunami crossed the Pacific to Hawaii in about this time, at deep-ocean speeds of ≈ 700 km/h.

tsunamiIsochrone8h.title: Tsunami arrival · ≈ 8 h
tsunamiIsochrone8h.description: Trans-oceanic reach. Even at this distance the wave can cause damage on the far coast (the 2004 Sumatra tsunami killed people in East Africa more than 8 hours after the rupture). DART buoys typically detect the front first.

tsunamiWaveAmplitude.title: Tsunami wave at this point
tsunamiWaveAmplitude.description: Tsunami wave height computed at this point in the ocean by the FMM simulation with Green's-law shoaling: includes real bathymetric refraction (the wave speeds up over deep ocean, slows over continental shelves and curves around coastlines). The arrow shows the local direction of propagation (∇T from the arrival-time field). Coastal run-ups and onshore inundation are described in the analysis panel, not on the map.

mmi9.title: MMI IX · violent shaking
mmi9.description: Even buildings designed to resist earthquakes suffer major damage. Underground pipes rupture, slopes shed landslides, foundations crack. Survival inside non-seismic construction is heavily compromised.

mmi8.title: MMI VIII · severe shaking
mmi8.description: Ordinary masonry buildings sustain considerable structural damage; poorly built ones may collapse. Heavy furniture shifts, chimneys and statues fall, people struggle to remain standing.

mmi7.title: MMI VII · strong shaking
mmi7.description: Hard to stand. Furniture moves and objects fall from shelves. Slight damage to well-built structures, moderate damage to fragile ones. Wall cracks and falling plaster are common.

pyroclasticRunout.title: Pyroclastic flow
pyroclasticRunout.description: A glowing avalanche of gas and ash at 600–1000 °C races down the volcano at hundreds of km/h. Anything in its path is incinerated or buried. You cannot outrun it — it is faster than a car on a motorway.

lateralBlast.title: Flank lateral blast
lateralBlast.description: A volcano flank collapses and releases a directional jet of pressurised gas and debris (as at Mt St Helens, 1980). It flattens forests and demolishes structures for tens of km — but only along the axis in which the flank gave way.

ashfallPlume.title: Ashfall · ≥ 1 mm
ashfallPlume.description: Volcanic ash deposits more than 1 mm thick. Under its own weight (when wet, almost as dense as wet sand) it collapses weak roofs, ruins crops, grounds aircraft and engines, and irritates lungs and eyes.

aftershock.title: Aftershock
aftershock.description: A follow-up rupture on the same fault. Aftershocks obey Omori's law: they are most frequent right after the mainshock and become rarer over time. A strong aftershock can cause as much damage as a standalone earthquake, especially on already-weakened buildings.

report.title: Simulation Report
report.noResult: No simulation has been evaluated yet. Run a scenario first, then return here to download the report.
report.equationsBody: The list below is filtered to the formulas this specific scenario actually exercised — an airburst cites Chyba & Popova, a megathrust cites Strasser and the tsunami chain, a surface burst cites Nordyke.
report.footer: Generated by Nimbus — Nuclear & Impact Modeling & Blast Understanding System · All formulas peer-reviewed · Open-source under Apache 2.0

about.title: Nimbus — Nuclear & Impact Modeling & Blast Understanding System
about.subtitle: Open-source · Peer-reviewed formulas
about.body: Every number on screen comes from a published formula — no simulated drama, no rounded-up showmanship. Pick an event, choose a location on the globe, and watch the crater, blast, thermal, seismic, and tsunami footprints render at true geographic scale.

glossary.title: Glossary of terms
glossary.subtitle: Plain-language definitions for the numbers the simulator reports.

overpressure.name: Overpressure
overpressure.definition: Air-pressure spike above ambient produced by a blast wave. 1 psi (≈ 6.9 kPa) breaks windows; 5 psi (≈ 34.5 kPa) collapses residential buildings.

thermalFluence.name: Thermal fluence
thermalFluence.definition: Total radiant energy per unit area received at the skin during the heat pulse of an explosion. Measured in J/m² or cal/cm²; threshold damage bands scale linearly with this quantity.

burn3rd.name: 3rd-degree burn radius
burn3rd.definition: Distance at which unshielded skin absorbs enough thermal fluence (≈ 8 cal/cm² = 3.35 × 10⁵ J/m²) to produce full-thickness burns. The simulator ignores atmospheric attenuation, so real reaches are 50–70 % of the unshielded value.

hopkinsonCranz.name: Hopkinson–Cranz scaling
hopkinsonCranz.definition: Cube-root yield scaling: the same overpressure appears at a range R ∝ W^(1/3) regardless of yield. Makes it possible to compare Hiroshima and Tsar Bomba on a single curve.

mw.name: Moment magnitude (Mw)
mw.definition: Logarithmic measure of earthquake size from the seismic moment: Mw = (2/3)·log₁₀(M₀) − 6.07, with M₀ in N·m. One unit of Mw ≈ 32× more energy.

seismicMoment.name: Seismic moment (M₀)
seismicMoment.definition: Fundamental scalar size of an earthquake: M₀ = μ · A · D where μ is rock rigidity, A the rupture area, and D the average slip. All other magnitude scales reduce to this at large events.

mmi.name: Modified Mercalli Intensity (MMI)
mmi.definition: Integer I–XII felt-intensity scale describing what people feel and what structures do. MMI VII = "strong", MMI VIII = "severe", MMI IX = "violent". Not the same as magnitude — it depends on distance and site.

pga.name: Peak Ground Acceleration (PGA)
pga.definition: Largest absolute horizontal acceleration the ground experiences during an earthquake, expressed in m/s² or g. Engineering codes map PGA to design requirements.

vei.name: Volcanic Explosivity Index (VEI)
vei.definition: Integer 0–8 ranking eruptions by bulk ejecta volume. VEI 5 = Mt St Helens 1980, VEI 6 = Krakatau 1883, VEI 7 = Tambora 1815, VEI 8 = supereruptions like Toba.

plinianColumn.name: Plinian column
plinianColumn.definition: Buoyant cloud of pumice, ash, and hot gas rising above a vent during the paroxysmal phase of a large eruption. Named for Pliny the Younger's description of Vesuvius 79 CE.

pyroclastic.name: Pyroclastic density current (PDC)
pyroclastic.definition: Hot, ground-hugging torrent of volcanic gas, ash, and fragments that moves at 100+ km/h and destroys everything in its path. Runout scales roughly as volume^(1/3).

dre.name: Dense-rock equivalent (DRE)
dre.definition: The volume an erupted deposit would occupy if you squeezed the air out — i.e. magma without pores or vesicles. Used to compare eruptions of very different porosities. ρ ≈ 2 500 kg/m³.

wardAsphaugCavity.name: Ward–Asphaug water cavity
wardAsphaugCavity.definition: Transient hole punched through the ocean when an asteroid hits water. Its collapse radiates the tsunami wavefront. Radius scales as E^(1/4), amplitude decays 1/r in the far field.

greensLaw.name: Green's law (shoaling)
greensLaw.definition: As a long wave climbs onto a shelf, its amplitude grows as (h_deep / h_shallow)^(1/4). A 1 m deep-ocean tsunami steepens to ≈ 4 m on a 15 m shelf.

hob.name: Height of burst (HOB)
hob.definition: Altitude at which a nuclear or chemical weapon is detonated. Surface bursts couple energy into the ground (cratering); optimum airbursts (~150–300 m·kt⁻¹ᐟ³) maximise the 5 psi reach via Mach reflection; very high bursts dissipate into the atmosphere.

machReflection.name: Mach reflection
machReflection.definition: When the angle between the incident shock front and the ground exceeds a critical value, the regular reflection collapses into a Mach stem — a single, stronger shock travelling along the ground. The optimum HOB is the altitude at which the Mach reflection sits at the design overpressure radius.

pancakeModel.name: Pancake fragmentation model
pancakeModel.definition: When a meteoroid's ram pressure (ρ_air · v²) exceeds its tensile strength Y, it fragments and the cloud spreads laterally as it descends — a 'pancake' that decelerates faster than an intact body and dumps most of its kinetic energy in the atmosphere.

breakupAltitude.name: Breakup altitude (h_b)
breakupAltitude.definition: Altitude at which fragmentation begins: h_b = H · ln(ρ₀ · v² / Y). For Chelyabinsk (17 m, 19 km/s, S-type) this is ≈ 38 km; for Chicxulub (15 km) it is ≈ 46 km. Note: fragmentation onset alone does NOT mean the body airbursts — see penetration bonus.

burstAltitude.name: Burst altitude
burstAltitude.definition: Altitude of peak energy deposition: h_burst = h_b − 2·H − penetrationBonus. When this is below the surface (large bodies) the simulator flags the regime as INTACT.

penetrationBonus.name: Penetration bonus
penetrationBonus.definition: Diameter-dependent depth (1.2 · ln(D/10) · H) that the pancake gains over a point source. For Chicxulub-class bodies it can exceed the breakup altitude itself, so the body never bursts in the atmosphere.

energyFractionToGround.name: Energy fraction to ground (gf)
energyFractionToGround.definition: Fraction of the impactor's kinetic energy that reaches the ground as cratering / seismic work. 1.0 for INTACT (Chicxulub), ≈ 0.02 for COMPLETE_AIRBURST (Tunguska, Chelyabinsk). The complement (1 − gf) is released in the atmosphere as flash + shock.

atmosphericYield.name: Atmospheric airburst yield
atmosphericYield.definition: Fraction of the kinetic energy released as a fireball + shock pulse during entry: E_atm = (1 − gf) · E_kinetic. For Chelyabinsk this is ≈ 0.33 Mt TNT-equivalent (98 % of total).

bolideAirburstAmplification.name: Bolide airburst amplification
bolideAirburstAmplification.definition: Closed-form altitude amplification f(h) = (P_0 / P_amb(h))^(1/β) with β = 5/3 (Sachs intermediate-shock exponent). Applied to the Kinney-Graham overpressure / Glasstone thermal radii of an airburst event. Built from Whitham (1974) weak-shock invariance, Sachs (1944) blast scaling, and the U.S. Standard Atmosphere 1976.

aftershockCatalogue.name: Aftershock catalogue
aftershockCatalogue.definition: Synthetic post-mainshock seismicity following Reasenberg-Jones / Båth / Omori-Utsu / Gutenberg-Richter. Deterministic given a seed, so the same scenario URL always produces the same sequence.

megathrust.name: Megathrust subduction interface
megathrust.definition: Shallow plate-boundary thrust fault at a subduction zone. Produces the largest earthquakes ever recorded (Mw 9–9.5: Tōhoku, Sumatra, Valdivia, Alaska) and the most basin-spanning tsunamis. Rupture-length scaling differs from continental crustal events.

vs30.name: Vs30
vs30.definition: Time-averaged shear-wave velocity in the upper 30 m of soil/rock at a site. NEHRP B-rock = 760 m/s (no amplification); soft alluvium = 180 m/s (~1.5× amplification). Wald & Allen 2007 derive Vs30 from topographic slope when no in-situ measurement is available.

liquefactionRadius.name: Liquefaction radius
liquefactionRadius.definition: Maximum ground range within which liquefaction is likely on saturated sandy soil. Threshold PGA scales with magnitude per Idriss 1999 MSF = (M/7.5)^(−2.56).

lateralBlast.name: Lateral blast (Mt St Helens-class)
lateralBlast.definition: Directional jet of pressurised gas + debris released when a volcano flank decompresses — flattens forests across a 180° sector for tens of km along the failure axis (Glicken 1996 archetype).

flankCollapse.name: Flank collapse / sector collapse
flankCollapse.definition: Catastrophic failure of a volcano's flank, sliding a sector of the edifice into the sea. Generates a local megatsunami via the Watts (2000) cavity-collapse mechanism. Anak Krakatau 22 Dec 2018 is the canonical instrumental case.

ashfallIsopach.name: Ashfall isopach
ashfallIsopach.definition: Contour line of constant tephra deposit thickness on the ground. The 1 mm isopach is the conventional reach for 'noticeable ashfall'; the 1 cm isopach defines the air-quality / aviation hazard envelope.

lahar.name: Lahar
lahar.definition: Saturated debris flow of volcanic origin — slurry of pyroclastic material + meltwater + entrained sediment moving at tens of km/h. The 1985 Nevado del Ruiz lahar (Armero tragedy, ≈ 23 000 fatalities) is the canonical reference.

tsunamiCelerity.name: Tsunami celerity
tsunamiCelerity.definition: Open-ocean propagation speed: c = √(g · h). At 4 km mean depth this is ≈ 198 m/s ≈ 713 km/h — the popular-science 'speed of a jet airliner'. On a 100 m continental shelf the wave slows to ≈ 31 m/s.

sourceWavelength.name: Tsunami source wavelength
sourceWavelength.definition: Spacing between successive crests at the source, set by the dominant Fourier mode: λ ≈ 2 × cavity diameter (impact / explosion) or 2 × rupture length (megathrust). For Tōhoku 2011 this is ≈ 1 400 km.

dominantPeriod.name: Dominant period
dominantPeriod.definition: Time between successive crests at the source: T = λ / c. For Tōhoku 2011 ≈ 7 000 s ≈ 2 h, consistent with the 30 min – 2 h range reported at coastal tide gauges.

synolakisRunup.name: Synolakis run-up
synolakisRunup.definition: Coastal-inundation height for a solitary long wave on a plane-slope beach: R_max = 2.831 · H · √(cot β) · (H/d)^(1/4). The simulator uses the actual DEM slope when available; otherwise the textbook 1:100 reference.

inundationDistance.name: Inland inundation distance
inundationDistance.definition: Geometric horizontal reach of the run-up wedge: L = R_runup / tan(β). On a 1:100 reference beach this is 100× the run-up; on a 1:30 dune face only 30×; on a 1:300 mud-flat 300×. Order-of-magnitude only.

damageTier.name: Tsunami damage tier
damageTier.definition: Six-tier coastal-damage classification keyed off run-up height: < 0.3 m tide-gauge only, 0.3–1 m light flooding, 1–3 m cars displaced, 3–6 m wood-frame destruction, 6–10 m concrete buildings damaged, > 10 m Tōhoku-/Lituya-class destruction.

beachSlopeFromDEM.name: Beach slope from on-site DEM
beachSlopeFromDEM.definition: When the user clicks on a coastal cell with a real terrain gradient, the simulator samples the AWS Terrarium DEM tile around the click and feeds the local slope (clamped to the [1:1000, 1:3] envelope) into the Synolakis run-up. Outside the envelope the formula falls back to the 1:100 reference.

populationExposure.name: Population exposure
populationExposure.definition: Sum of population-grid cells inside the headline damage radius. Backed by the WorldPop 2020 1 km mosaic (Tatem 2017) or the JRC GHSL R2023A grid (Schiavina 2023), fetched as Cloud-Optimised GeoTIFFs via HTTP Range requests. Population exposed ≠ casualties.

vulnerabilityFunction.name: Vulnerability function (intentionally not implemented)
vulnerabilityFunction.definition: Maps a hazard intensity (overpressure, MMI, runup) to a probability of fatality / injury. Glasstone §12 lethality bands for blast, Wald & Quitoriano (1999) PAGER fragility curves for shaking, FEMA P-646 §3 for tsunami. Intentionally omitted from Nimbus to avoid the 'rainbow numbers' effect — the figure surfaced is exposure, not casualties.

citations.kineticEnergy: Kinetic energy E = ½ m v² (Collins, Melosh & Marcus 2005, Eq. 3). Mass from a spherical impactor of the chosen bulk density.
citations.finalCrater: Final crater rim-to-rim diameter from the π-group scaling and simple/complex branch (Collins, Melosh & Marcus 2005, Eqs. 21, 22, 27).
citations.transientCrater: Transient crater diameter from π-group scaling (Collins, Melosh & Marcus 2005, Eq. 21). The transient crater is the cavity at maximum excavation, before gravitational modification.
citations.craterDepth: Depth-to-diameter ratio d/D ≈ 1/5 for simple craters; complex craters scale from Pike (1980) piecewise relation.
citations.ejecta: Continuous ejecta-blanket thickness T(r) = 0.14 · R · (R/r)³ (McGetchin et al. 1973; Collins et al. 2005 Eq. 28). Outer edges invert the same formula at the quoted threshold.
citations.firestorm: Thermal-pulse ignition and firestorm thresholds from Glasstone & Dolan (1977) §7.40–§7.42. Impact luminous efficiency η ≈ 3 × 10⁻³ from Toon et al. (1997); nuclear thermal partition 0.35.
citations.atmosphere: Stratospheric sub-µm dust mass linearly scaled against Toon et al. (1997) Table 3; shock-atmospheric HNO₃ linearly scaled against Prinn & Fegley (1987); climate tier by kinetic-energy band.
citations.seismicMagnitude: Seismic-equivalent moment magnitude Mw = 0.67·log₁₀(E) − 5.87 (Schultz & Gault 1975, as used by Collins et al. 2005 Eq. 31).
citations.seismicMagnitudeTW: Modern impact-seismicity estimator from Teanby & Wookey (2011) EPSL 303: M₀ = k·E with seismic efficiency k ≈ 10⁻⁴, then Mw via Hanks–Kanamori (1979). Runs 2–3 Mw units below Schultz-Gault — a more defensible upper bound.
citations.tsunamiCavity: Water-column cavity radius R_C = (3·E / (2π·ρ_w·g))^(1/4) from the Ward & Asphaug (2000) energy-partitioning form.
citations.tsunamiFarField: Far-field amplitude A(r) = A₀·R_C/r (Ward & Asphaug 2000) — 1/r geometric decay of a shallow-water gravity wave.
citations.tsunamiWunnemann: Hydrocode-corrected far field (Wünnemann, Weiss & Hofmann 2007, M&PS 42; Melosh 2003). Short-wavelength impact waves dissipate faster than classical tsunamis, lowering Ward–Asphaug amplitudes by 4–10× at continent-crossing range.
citations.synolakisRunup: Coastal run-up on a 1:100 plane beach with 10 m offshore depth (Synolakis 1987, JFM 185 523–545). R = 2.831·H·√(cot β)·(H/d)^(1/4). Illustrative inundation height — real coastal run-up depends on bathymetry, harbour geometry, and breaking regime.
citations.tsunamiDispersion: Frequency-dependent dispersion correction to the far-field amplitude (Heidarzadeh & Satake 2015 GJI 202 / JGR Oceans 120). Exponential decay with ~2 500 km scale length beyond the Wünnemann/Ward 1/r envelope — matches DART-buoy records of Sumatra 2004 and Tōhoku 2011.
citations.seismicMoment: Seismic moment M₀ = 10^(1.5·Mw + 9.1) N·m — Hanks & Kanamori (1979) SI form of the moment-magnitude scale.
citations.ruptureLength: Surface rupture length L = 10^(a + b·Mw) km — Wells & Coppersmith (1994) empirical regression by fault slip regime.
citations.mmi: Modified Mercalli Intensity from Joyner & Boore (1981) PGA attenuation combined with Worden et al. (2012) piecewise GMICE for California.
citations.ngaWest2: Modern PGA estimator from Boore, Stewart, Seyhan & Atkinson (2014), Earthquake Spectra 30(3) 1057–1085 — NGA-West2 BSSA14. Replaces the 1981-era Joyner–Boore fit with magnitude-dependent geometric spreading, 4.5 km near-source clamp, fault-type constants, and Vs30 site amplification.
citations.mmiEurope: European PGA→MCS conversion from Faenza & Michelini (2010), GJI 180 1117–1133. Italian ShakeMap calibration; prefer this to the Worden (California) fit for events on the Eurasian plate.
citations.strasser: Megathrust rupture-length scaling from Strasser, Arango & Bommer (2010), SRL 81(6). Fits 95 subduction-interface events; used instead of Wells–Coppersmith for subduction-zone thrusts, where W&C over-predicts at Mw ≥ 8.
citations.liquefaction: Liquefaction-potential threshold radius on susceptible saturated sandy soil. PGA threshold 0.10 g at Mw 7.5, scaled by Idriss (1999) MSF = (M/7.5)^(−2.56). Reference: Youd & Idriss (2001) ASCE JGGE.
citations.waldAllen: Site Vs30 derived from the topographic slope at the scenario coordinates (Wald & Allen 2007 Table 1, active-tectonic regions). Rock outcrops → Vs30 ≈ 760 m/s (NEHRP B); alluvial basins → Vs30 ≈ 180 m/s (NEHRP E). Echoes the user override when one was set, otherwise the rock reference.
citations.vei: Volcanic Explosivity Index from Newhall & Self (1982), integer 0–8 binned by bulk ejecta volume.
citations.plumeHeight: Plinian-plume height H = 2.00 · V̇^0.241 km (Mastin et al. 2009, Eq. 1) from the volume eruption rate.
citations.pyroclasticRunout: Pyroclastic-density-current reach L = 10 · V^(1/3) km (H/L ≈ 0.1 mobility, Sheridan 1979).
citations.pdcEnergyLine: Upper-bound PDC runout from Dade & Huppert (1998) Geology 26(9) / Hayashi & Self (1992): L = H_plume / slope with slope ≈ 0.10 for dense flows.
citations.climateCooling: VEI-scaled stratospheric-aerosol climate forcing. ΔT doubles per unit VEI from the El Chichón 1982 / Pinatubo 1991 / Tambora 1815 record, per Robock (2000) Rev. Geophys. 38 and Sato et al. (1993) GISS AOD dataset.
citations.ashfall: 1 mm isopach area ~ 3 000 · V^0.8 km². Simplified Walker (1980) / Pyle (1989) exponential-thinning fit; real ashfall is wind-shaped.
citations.lahar: Saturated debris-flow runout L = 0.05 · V^0.38 km (V in m³). Iverson (1997) / Vallance & Iverson (2015). Reproduces Mt St Helens 1980 within a factor of 2.
citations.blast: Peak overpressure inverted via Kinney–Graham (1985) surface-burst fit; thresholds at 5 psi (building collapse) and 1 psi (window breakage) from Glasstone & Dolan (1977) §5.129/§5.139.
citations.hob: Height-of-burst correction applied over the Kinney–Graham baseline — piecewise fit to Fig. 3.73 of Glasstone & Dolan (1977) and Fig. 3-3 of Needham (2018), Blast Waves, Springer. 0.85× at surface, 1.0× at optimum (≈150–300 m·kt⁻¹ᐟ³), collapses for stratospheric bursts.
citations.radiation: Initial gamma + neutron lethal-dose radii. LD₅₀/60 ≈ 4.5 Gy, LD₁₀₀ ≈ 8 Gy, ARS threshold ≈ 1 Gy. Radii scale as yield^0.4, anchored on the 1 kt reference from Glasstone & Dolan (1977) Fig. 8.46; dose-response from UNSCEAR 2000 Annex C / BEIR VII (2006).
citations.emp: Electromagnetic-pulse regime classification. Low-altitude bursts are EMP-negligible at ground level; high-altitude (> 30 km) bursts produce the canonical 50 kV/m HEMP per IEC 61000-2-9, with ground footprint equal to the horizon tangent disc. Longmire (1978) IEEE AP-26 is the underlying current-sheet model.
citations.thermal: Unshielded third-degree-burn radius at the 8 cal/cm² fluence threshold (Glasstone & Dolan 1977, Table 7.41). Real reach shrinks to 50–70 % once atmospheric attenuation is applied.
citations.nuclearCrater: Apparent surface-burst crater diameter D = K · W_kt^0.3 (Nordyke 1977), with K tied to the ground type — hard rock, firm ground, dry soil, wet coral.
citations.atmosphericEntry: Fragmentation-onset altitude h_b = H · ln(ρ₀v² / Y), where dynamic pressure first exceeds the impactor's tensile strength Y (Chyba et al. 1993, Nature 361: 40; Collins et al. 2005, M&PS 40, Eq. 9). Burst altitude is set 2 scale heights below h_b, minus a diameter-dependent penetration bonus that lets large bodies sail through the atmosphere intact.
citations.bolideAirburstAmplification: Closed-form altitude amplification f(h) = (P_0 / P_amb(h))^(1/β) with β = 5/3 (Sachs intermediate-shock exponent). Built from Whitham (1974) §8.2 weak-shock invariance ΔP/P_amb ≈ const through stratified atmosphere, Sachs (1944) blast scaling ΔP ∝ R^(−β), and the U.S. Standard Atmosphere 1976 (NOAA-S/T 76-1562) for actual P(h). Capped at 15× for stratospheric scenarios. Validates within ~1 % on Chelyabinsk 2013 (Brown et al. 2013 Nature 503: 238) and within ~15 % on Tunguska 1908.
